name: Directorio sismo
db_description:
I built a repository of all resources that I've found, developed by citizens to accelerate search and rescue and provide help.

Time is of the essence, so information needs to be centralized in one place. Venezuelans need access to a single platform to find whatever they need at the moment.

analysis.summary: Venezuela Solidaria ("Directorio Sismo") is a single static HTML page (index.html, ~1570 lines of inline CSS/HTML/JS) that acts as a curated link directory pointing to ~40+ external third-party sites/forms/spreadsheets for earthquake relief (hospitals, missing persons, pet rescue, engineers, donations). It has no backend, no database, no framework, and no build system — it is hand-edited HTML deployed directly to Netlify, plus a client-side-only admin.html panel with a hardcoded plaintext password.
analysis.project_type: Static informational directory / link-aggregator website (no application logic)

infra_deploy: Netlify (Netlify Forms for two contact/report forms, referenced Netlify hostname)

architecture.pattern: None — single static page with inline styles/scripts; two other standalone static pages (admin.html, gracias.html)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — no database of any kind; all directory entries are hardcoded <div> cards directly in index.html markup
architecture.api_design: None — no API endpoints. 'Data' is either static HTML markup or two Netlify Forms (data-netlify="true") that POST to gracias.html
architecture.separation_of_concerns: None — HTML, CSS, and JS are all inlined into single monolithic files; content and presentation are fully intermixed
architecture.modularity: Zero modularity; no components, no reusable templates, no build tooling (no package.json, no bundler)
notable_patterns: Client-side-only 'admin panel' (admin.html) with a hardcoded plaintext password (const ADMIN_PASSWORD = "Venezuela2025!") checked in the browser and 'session' tracked via sessionStorage — trivially bypassable and the password is publicly visible in the repo
Simple client-side EN/ES i18n toggle using data-en attributes and localStorage
Netlify Forms used for two of the several dozen entry points instead of any real submission/moderation pipeline

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat: just index.html, admin.html, gracias.html, og-image.jpg, README.txt at repo root — no src/, no folders, no config files
code_organization.naming_quality: Reasonable Spanish naming of CSS classes (tarjeta, categoria-grupo, filtros) but everything lives in one giant inline file
code_organization.documentation_quality: README.txt is a 15-line non-technical note explaining which files exist, aimed at a non-developer site owner, not real project documentation
code_organization.reasoning: No component structure, no build system, no separation between markup/style/logic; this is a hand-maintained static page, not an engineered codebase.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: A plaintext admin password is committed directly in admin.html source code and visible to anyone viewing the public GitHub repo or page source
production_readiness.security_notes: Hardcoded client-side password check is not real authentication (viewable/bypassable via browser devtools); no server-side validation of any kind since there is no server
production_readiness.scalability_notes: As a static page it 'scales' trivially via Netlify CDN, but has no capacity to actually process data, verify reports, or handle load beyond serving static markup
production_readiness.reasoning: There is no application logic to secure or scale — it is a link list with a fake admin login exposing a real (if weak) secret in source control.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real but minimal — actively maintained content (38 commits, all 'Update index.html', pushed recently) curating genuinely useful external links, but not software in any meaningful engineering sense
maturity.reasoning: The content is clearly being kept up to date by a real person post-earthquake, but the artifact itself is a single static HTML file with no tests, CI, or build tooling of any kind.

viability.reasoning: As a curated resource directory it could have real value directing victims to other (more capable) tools, but the repo itself contains no unique functionality, no data layer, and a fake admin panel with an exposed secret; it is not a deployable 'solution' so much as a link page that duplicates functionality found in the tools it links to.

key_features: Categorized directory of ~40+ external earthquake relief resources (health, missing persons, pets, engineers/structural inspection, donations, shelters)
Client-side search/filter and category tabs over the static list
Donations modal listing 16 manually-curated verified donation recipients with payment details
Two Netlify Forms for visitor comments/suggestions
Fake client-side 'admin panel' with hardcoded password for site owner to reference edit instructions
analysis.merge_potential: Low as a codebase to merge with others technically, but the curated link list itself (verified donation orgs, resource URLs) could be valuable content to fold into a more capable, database-backed directory/aggregator project from the same hackathon.
red_flags: Hardcoded plaintext admin password committed to a public repo (const ADMIN_PASSWORD = "Venezuela2025!" in admin.html), with a fake client-side-only login
No backend, database, tests, or CI — it is a single static HTML file, not application software

All 'directory data' is hardcoded HTML markup requiring manual code edits (via GitHub web UI, per commit history) to update, with no moderation/verification pipeline for the many external third-party links it aggregates

gaps: No screenshots asset confirmed in repo/press kit
No real-user testimonials or usage metrics
Technical shallowness (single static HTML file, exposed hardcoded admin password) undermines a 'built a platform' narrative — better framed as a curated resource hub
diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame as 'a curated, actively-updated directory that helps Venezuelans find every earthquake relief resource in one place' rather than as a technical platform — lead with the value to victims, not the engineering.

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch on https://directorio-sismo.netlify.app/ succeeded and shows a live, actively maintained page with categorized tabs (Health, Emergencies, Missing Persons, Pets, Engineers, Donations, Shelters), search/filter, a verified donations section (17 orgs), volunteer registration, and bilingual EN/ES content, last updated the same day. It reads as a real, currently-useful resource for victims, not a placeholder landing page.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: Directorio Sismo: un solo lugar con todos los recursos verificados post-terremoto en Venezuela — hospitales, personas desaparecidas, mascotas, ingenieros y donaciones confiables.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake information is scattered across dozens of ad-hoc Google Forms, spreadsheets, and social media posts; a single curated, categorized, bilingual directory genuinely reduces the time and cognitive load for victims and volunteers trying to find help (hospitals, missing-persons trackers, shelters, verified donation channels). This addresses a real coordination/discoverability problem in the immediate aftermath of a disaster, though it does not itself provide any new capability beyond aggregating and organizing existing third-party resources.
